text: What is the output of the following code snippet?

options.text: The code does not compile because one of the assignments requires an explicit numeric cast.

explanation: star = 2 + (2*3) = 8. 8 > 10 is false so sun = 3.0f. jupiter = (3.0f + 9) - 1.0f = 11.0. --moon = 8, 8 <= 8 is true so mars = 2. Output: 3.0, 11.0, 2.

text: What is the output of the following application?
explanation: ++x makes x=6, then 6*3=18, then --x makes x=5, 18/5=3 (integer division truncates), 3+10=13. Final: x=5, y=13.

text: Which of the following is true about the Integer cache in Java?

options.text: true, true — all Integer values are cached.
false, false — Integer objects are never the same reference.
true, false — Integer caches values from -128 to 127.
false, true — Integer caches values from 128 onward.

explanation: The JVM caches Integer objects for values -128 through 127. a and b reference the same cached object, so == is true. c and d are outside the cache range and create new objects, so == is false. Always use .equals() to compare wrapper values.

text: What is the result of the following code?

options.text: Compilation error — cannot assign double to byte.
Compilation error — 5.5 is a double literal.
b has the value 15 — compound assignment includes an implicit cast.
b has the value 15.5 — byte is promoted to double.

explanation: Compound assignment operators (+=, -=, etc.) include an implicit narrowing cast. b += 5.5 is equivalent to b = (byte)(b + 5.5). 10 + 5.5 = 15.5, cast to byte = 15.

text: What is the output of the following code?
code:
System.out.println(1 + 2 + "3");
System.out.println("1" + 2 + 3);
explanation: Evaluation is left-to-right. 1+2=3 (numeric), then 3+"3"="33" (string concat). For the second line, "1"+2="12" (concat), then "12"+3="123" (concat). Output: 33 then 123.
source: Oracle Docs – Expressions, Statements, and Blocks: https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/java/nutsandbolts/expressions.html

text: What happens when the following code is executed?
code:
Integer x = null;
int y = x;

options.text: y is assigned 0 — null unboxes to the default int value.
Compilation error — null cannot be assigned to Integer.
y is assigned -1 — null unboxes to -1 for numeric types.
Compilation error — Integer cannot be assigned to int.

explanation: Unboxing a null Integer reference throws NullPointerException at runtime. The compiler allows the assignment because it inserts an automatic unboxing call (x.intValue()), but calling a method on null causes NPE.

text: Which of the following statements about numeric promotion are correct? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: byte and short operands are promoted to int before a binary operation.
If one operand is long and the other is int, the int is promoted to long.
char operands are promoted to short before a binary operation.
If one operand is float and the other is long, the long is promoted to float.
Two int operands always produce an int result, even when the value overflows.

explanation: A: byte and short (and char) are promoted to int. B: mixed int/long promotes int to long. C is wrong — char is promoted to int, not short. D: mixed float/long promotes long to float. E: integer arithmetic always stays within the declared type and overflows silently.

text: What is the output of the following code?
code:
System.out.println(Math.round(3.5));
System.out.println(Math.round(-3.5));

options.text: The code does not compile — Math.round returns double.

explanation: Math.round() rounds half-up toward positive infinity. 3.5 rounds to 4. -3.5 rounds toward positive infinity, so it rounds to -3 (not -4). Math.round(double) returns long; Math.round(float) returns int.

text: What is the output of the following code?

options.text: Compilation error — toUpperCase() is not defined on String.

explanation: String is immutable. toUpperCase() returns a new String with the uppercase value but does not modify the original. Since the return value is not assigned, s still refers to "Hello".

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: String literals with the same content share the same pool object, so a == b is true. new String(...) always creates a new heap object outside the pool, so a == c is false. equals() compares content, so a.equals(c) is true.

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: StringBuilder is mutable. append() modifies the object in place and returns this. After appending ", World", the content is "Hello, World" which has 12 characters.

text: Which of the following text block declarations is valid?

options.text: Options A and D are both valid.

explanation: A text block must start with """ followed immediately by a line terminator — content cannot begin on the same line as the opening """. A, D are invalid for this reason. B ends on the same line as the opening so also invalid. C is the only valid form.

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation:
After incidental whitespace stripping, the content is "red
green
blue
" — 3 words (3+5+4 = 12 chars) plus 3 newlines = 15 characters total. The closing """ on its own line causes a trailing newline to be included.

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: 2024 is a leap year (divisible by 4), so February has 29 days. When adding one month to January 31, the result is adjusted to the last valid day of February — Feb 29 in a leap year.

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: LocalTime wraps around midnight. 23:30 plus 2 hours crosses midnight and wraps to 01:30. LocalTime has no concept of dates so it does not overflow — it simply wraps.

text: What is the result of the following code?
explanation: Period.between() returns separate year/month/day components, not a total count. From Jan 1 to Jun 15 is 5 months and 14 days (Jan→Feb=1, Feb→Mar=2, Mar→Apr=3, Apr→May=4, May→Jun=5, then 1→15 = 14 days). getMonths()=5, getDays()=14.

text: What is the result of applying a Duration to a LocalDate?
explanation: Duration is time-based (seconds and nanoseconds) and cannot be applied to a date-based type like LocalDate. Calling date.plus(Duration.ofDays(1)) throws UnsupportedTemporalTypeException at runtime. Use Period.ofDays(1) instead.

text: A developer needs to convert a ZonedDateTime from America/New_York to Europe/London preserving the same moment in time. Which method should be used?

options.text: nyTime.toInstant().atZone(ZoneId.of("Europe/London")) — but only withZoneSameInstant is correct API

explanation: withZoneSameInstant() converts the ZonedDateTime to another zone keeping the same underlying Instant — the same moment in time. withZoneSameLocal() keeps the same local clock reading but in a different zone, changing the moment. Note: toInstant().atZone() also works but is not among the clean choices here.

text: Which of the following statements about the Math API are correct? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: Math.abs(Integer.MIN_VALUE) returns a positive value equal to Integer.MAX_VALUE + 1.
Math.pow(x, y) always returns a double.
Math.random() returns a value in the range [0.0, 1.0), including 0.0 but excluding 1.0.
Math.addExact(Integer.MAX_VALUE, 1) returns Integer.MIN_VALUE due to overflow.

explanation: A is wrong — Math.abs(Integer.MIN_VALUE) overflows and returns Integer.MIN_VALUE (negative). B is correct — Math.pow always returns double. C is correct — Math.random() includes 0.0 but excludes 1.0. D is correct — ceil rounds toward +∞, so -3.1 → -3.0. E is wrong — Math.addExact throws ArithmeticException on overflow rather than wrapping.

text: What is the output of the following code?
code:
String s = "Hello, World!";
System.out.println(s.substring(7, 12));
explanation: substring(beginIndex, endIndex) is beginIndex inclusive and endIndex exclusive. Index 7 is 'W', index 12 (exclusive) stops before '!'. Characters at indices 7,8,9,10,11 are 'W','o','r','l','d' → "World".

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: Integer division 5/2 = 2 (truncates). 5 % 2 = 1. Output is '2 1'.

text: What is the output of the following code?
code:
double d = 1.0 / 0;
System.out.println(d);
explanation: Dividing a floating-point number by zero produces Infinity, not an exception. ArithmeticException only occurs with integer division by zero.

text: Which of the following are valid text block declarations? (Choose all that apply.)
explanation: Text blocks require the opening triple-quote followed immediately by a newline. B is invalid (no newline after opening """). C is valid Java but the closing """ has no newline indent. A and D are valid.

text: What is the result of Math.round(3.5) and Math.round(-3.5)?
explanation: Math.round uses half-up rounding. 3.5 rounds to 4. -3.5 rounds to -3 (toward positive infinity when exactly .5).

text: Which of the following correctly parses the date '2025-06-15' using the Date-Time API?
explanation: LocalDate.parse() accepts ISO-8601 format (yyyy-MM-dd) directly. LocalDate.of() takes int parameters. Date.parse() is deprecated. LocalDate.from() takes a TemporalAccessor, not a String.

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: String literals are interned — a and b point to the same pool object, so == is true. new String() creates a new heap object, so a == c is false even though the contents are equal.

text: What does Period.between(LocalDate.of(2024,1,1), LocalDate.of(2025,3,15)) return?
explanation: Period.between measures date-based amounts: 1 year, 2 months, 14 days. It does not convert to total days (that would be Duration or ChronoUnit.DAYS).

text: What is the output of the following code?
code:
String s = "  hello world  ";
System.out.println(s.strip().replace(" ", "-"));
explanation: strip() removes leading and trailing whitespace (Unicode-aware, unlike trim()). replace(' ', '-') replaces the space between 'hello' and 'world'. Result: 'hello-world'.

text: What does the following code print?
explanation: insert(int offset, String) inserts at the given index. Index 2 is between 'a' (index 1) and 'v' (index 2), so '-' is inserted between 'Ja' and 'va' → 'Ja-va'.
